references.title: Key factors predicting  problem-based learning in online  environments: Evidence from  multimodal learning analytics

extracted.Courses: 1 Online Course in Social Work and Problem-Solving
extracted.Data sources: Student Reports, Discussion Forums, LMS logs, Questionnaires
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data was collected from Online Discussion Forums in 2020 and 2021 and each post was subsequently encoded as either relevant for course or not relevant. 

Discussion posts themselves were encoded using different NLP techniques: TF-IDF, Word2Vec & Doc2Vec.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {AUC: 0.880 - SVM D2V, Cohen’s Kappa: 0.816 - SVM D2V}
extracted.Cluster Description:
Relçevant messages were labelled into 4 PBL-steps:

Representation-Relevant, Solution-Relevant, Justification-relevant & Evaluation-relevant
extracted.Implications: Students are more likely to engage in discussion in discussion with their peers than any other activity (i.e. answering questions). Educators hsould make an effort in fostering an environment where helping peers is rewarded.
extracted.Comments:
Participation of study was voluntary.

ML models ware meant to measure whether a specific forum entry is relevant or irrelevant for the course → which can be used as a hallmark of engagement of procrastinatory behavior. 

The predictions of this model are subsequently used as inputs for the Hierarchical Linear Regression.
extracted.Limitations:
Selection bias → only includes learners who have signed in to participate in a specific course.

Small sample size (student-wise and number of courses-wise) should be addressed in future works.

extracted.Courses: 1 Online Course in Social Work and Problem-Solving
extracted.Data sources: Student Reports, Discussion Forums, LMS logs, Questionnaires
extracted.Features: [Gender, Prior Knowledge, Self-Regulation, Posted, Replied, Words, Representation-rlvt, Solution-rlvt, Justification-rlvt, Evaluation-rlvt]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The predictions of the ML classifiers are used as features for this model. 

The idea is to identify which steps or phases of the PBL process are relevant towards better performance.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {Adjsuted R-Squared: 0.682 - Regression using all features, R-Squared: 0.713 - Al Features}
extracted.Cluster Description:
Results show that the predictions obtained from the ML models are better representations of peer-learning than exclusively using logs. 

Self-Regulation and peer-learning variables were found to be relevant predictors of PBL performance.
extracted.Implications:
Adequate labelling consistency from human evaluators may contribute to creating models able to predict engagement. 

Multi-modal learning analytics may be used to keep track of the stages of the PBL proces learners are into and provide adequate supoport to them.
extracted.Comments: Hierarchical linear regression results show that the addition of the peer-learning variables obtained from training the different ML models results into better overall predictions of performance in PBL than common constructs such as prior knowledge or gender.
extracted.Limitations: Selection bias → only includes learners who have signed in to participate.Small sample size (student-wise and number of courses-wise) should be addressed in future works.

annotations.text: Multimodal learning analytics has garnered a lot of interest among Problem-Based Learning researchers. Within this context, there is a gap in using text data generated from learner interactions with their peers. The authors explor MMLA on a sample of 104 fourth-year students to classify text in online discussions. Self-Regulation, messages and engagement were found to be predictive of online PBL performance.
